paper_title: Valve involvement in infective endocarditis among intravenous drug users: a systematic review and meta-analysis (Le et al., BMC Infectious Diseases, 2026)
source: User-supplied PDF (pymupdf4llm-style manual read), not PMC JATS
reviewer: claude (interactive session, not automated API call)
caveat:
Manual/interactive review of a user-supplied PDF outside the JAMA Network Open n=100 sample. References resolved to DOIs via Crossref bibliographic search (ref_resolver.py), abstracts fetched via Crossref/OpenAlex/PubMed (add_reference.py-style merge), same Gate-1 methodology as the JAMA audits. Covers all citation-bearing claims in the Introduction, Rationale, Methods, and Discussion sections (64 checked citation-instances); the Results section's citations to the paper's own 14 included studies [16-29] were not individually re-audited since those are the meta-analysis's own primary data reporting, not literature claims being cited for support.

GATE 2 (full-text) WAS RUN for this file, correcting an initial oversight -- every non-SUPPORTED Gate-1 verdict triggered an Unpaywall OA lookup (fulltext_fetch.py) and, where a legal full-text copy could be retrieved, a re-check against the actual paper rather than just the abstract, per docs/adjudication-criteria.md's 2-gate design. Of 13 unique references behind the flagged claims, full text was successfully retrieved for 4 (ref 4/Clarelin via direct PDF, ref 7/Leungsuwan via direct PDF, ref 6/Shmueli and ref 39/Parashar via PMC JATS). This produced 10 verdict upgrades from PARTIALLY_SUPPORTED to SUPPORTED -- most strikingly for ref 4 (Clarelin), where every one of 7 flagged claims (streptococcal/enterococcal proportions, surgery rates 42%/5.5%, 5-year survival 55%/84%, embolism risk, rising-LSIE-trend framing, and the S. aureus 85%/46% split) turned out to be exact matches to the paper's own Results tables -- none were in the abstract, so Gate-1-only checking would have produced 7 false 'unsupported' flags on a paper that was actually cited with high precision. This is direct empirical confirmation of why Gate 2 exists (see the 3-paper calibration's earlier finding of the same pattern). Full text was NOT retrievable for the remaining 9 references: refs 1, 3, 8, 32, and 36 are not open access per Unpaywall (fails closed, as designed); refs 2 and 5 are marked OA by Unpaywall but the direct-PDF fetch hit a landing/bot-check page and fails closed rather than guessing; refs 30 and 35 have PMC records but PMC's OA API returned only front-matter metadata with no <body> content (publisher restricts full-text distribution through PMC despite Unpaywall listing OA availability). Flagged claims tied to these 9 references remain at their original Gate-1 (abstract-only) verdict.

Two significant findings survive Gate 2 unchanged: (1) the reference list contains 2 duplicate entries -- ref 4 and ref 34 are the identical paper (Clarelin et al., Sci Rep 2021), and ref 26 and ref 32 are the identical paper (Huang et al., Infection 2020), each cited under two different numbers; (2) one likely wrong-reference-number citation -- the sentence 'The incidence of IE among IVDUs has increased substantially in parallel with the opioid epidemic [4]' cites a cross-sectional registry-comparison paper with no trend/opioid-epidemic content (confirmed absent in ref 4's full text too), while ref 1 (Khayata et al.)'s abstract opens with almost the identical sentence, strongly suggesting the citation number should have been 1, not 4. Also still standing: one internally contradicted co-citation (ref 36/Emara: 'multi-organ failure' claimed but abstract reports no significant difference in heart failure -- ref 36 is not OA so this couldn't be double-checked against full text), and ref 39/Parashar's topic-mismatch flag was confirmed by full text (a Vancouver HIV/ED-use cohort study, genuinely unrelated to IE).

items.sentence: Infective endocarditis (IE) is a severe infection of the endocardial surface, often affecting one or more heart valves [1].
items.ref_text: Khayata M, Hackney N, Addoumieh A, et al. Impact of opioid epidemic on infective endocarditis outcomes in the United States: from the national readmission database. Am J Cardiol. 2022;183:137-42.
items.abstract: Infective endocarditis (IE) is associated with marked morbidity and mortality in the United States and parallels the opioid pandemic. Few studies explore this interaction and its effect on clinical outcomes. We analyzed contemporary patients admitted with IE to determine predictors of readmission in the United States. The 2017 National Readmission Database was used... Of 40,413 index admissions for IE, 5,558 patients (13.8%) were readmitted within 30 days... opioid abuse (23.9% vs 15%, p<0.001)... [readmission risk factor study]
items.note: Ref 1 (Khayata) is a 30-day-readmission risk-factor study using the National Readmission Database -- its abstract has no connection to the basic definitional claim about IE affecting heart valves. Likely wrong reference for this sentence.

items.sentence: Despite improvements in antimicrobial therapy and cardiac surgery, IE remains associated with high morbidity and mortality worldwide [1].
items.ref_text: Khayata M, Hackney N, Addoumieh A, et al. Impact of opioid epidemic on infective endocarditis outcomes in the United States: from the national readmission database. Am J Cardiol. 2022;183:137-42.

items.sentence: Its incidence ranges from 3 to 10 cases per 100,000 person years [2].
items.ref_text: Baddour LM, Wilson WR, Bayer AS, et al. Infective endocarditis in adults: diagnosis, antimicrobial therapy, and management of complications: a scientific statement from the American Heart Association. Circulation. 2015;132(15):1435-86.
items.abstract: Infective endocarditis is a potentially lethal disease that has undergone major changes in both host and pathogen. The epidemiology of infective endocarditis has become more complex with today's myriad healthcare-associated factors that predispose to infection. Moreover, changes in pathogen prevalence, in particular a more common staphylococcal origin, have affected outcomes... This statement updates the 2005 iteration... [AHA scientific statement on diagnosis/therapy/management]
items.note: Ref 2 (Baddour, AHA statement) is a broad diagnosis/therapy/complications statement; abstract doesn't state this specific incidence figure.

items.sentence: Its incidence ranges from 3 to 10 cases per 100,000 person years [3].
items.ref_text: Cahill TJ, Baddour LM, Habib G, et al. Challenges in Infective Endocarditis. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2017;69(3):325-44.
items.abstract: Infective endocarditis is defined by a focus of infection within the heart and is a feared disease across the field of cardiology. It is frequently acquired in the health care setting, and more than one-half of cases now occur in patients without known heart disease. Despite optimal care, mortality approaches 30% at 1 year... Staphylococcus aureus, which has become the predominant causative organism in the developed world... There is a lack of research infrastructure and funding... Longstanding controversies such as the timing of surgery or the role of antibiotic prophylaxis have not been resolved.
items.note: Ref 3 (Cahill) abstract doesn't state this incidence figure either.

items.sentence: Clinical presentations may include non-specific symptoms such as fever and malaise, along with more characteristic findings such as new heart murmurs or embolic events [2].

items.sentence: The incidence of IE among IVDUs has increased substantially in parallel with the opioid epidemic [4].
items.ref_text: Clarelin A, Rasmussen M, Olaison L, Ragnarsson S. Comparing right-and left sided injection-drug related infective endocarditis. Sci Rep. 2021;11(1):1177.
items.abstract: The aim of the study was to compare background characteristics, microbiology and outcome of patients with right-sided and left-sided intravenous drug use (IDU) associated infective endocarditis (IE). A nationwide retrospective study using the Swedish Registry on Infective Endocarditis between 2008 and 2019 was conducted. A total of 586 people with IDU-IE were identified and divided into left-sided (n=204) and right-sided (n=382) IE. The mean age of patients in the left-sided group was 46 years compared to 35 years in the right-sided group, p<0.001. Left-sided IE had a higher proportion of females. Staphylococcus aureus was the causative pathogen in 48% of cases in the left-sided group compared to 88% in the right-sided group. Unadjusted and adjusted long-term survival was better in right-sided IE compared to left-sided IE.
items.note: Ref 4 (Clarelin) is a cross-sectional Swedish-registry comparison of right- vs left-sided IE characteristics -- it does not address incidence trends or the opioid epidemic at all. Notably, ref 1 (Khayata)'s abstract opens with almost this exact sentence ('IE is associated with...and parallels the opioid pandemic') -- strong circumstantial evidence this citation should have been ref 1, not ref 4.

items.sentence: Right-sided IE (RSIE), especially tricuspid valve involvement, remains the most common presentation in this group [4].

items.note: Ref 4's abstract confirms right-sided n=382 (65%) vs left-sided n=204 (35%) in their 586-patient Swedish registry -- right-sided is indeed the majority.

items.sentence: RSIE makes up 5–10% of all IE cases, and the tricuspid valve is involved in as many as 90% of RSIE presentations [5].

items.abstract: [No usable abstract available -- ESC guideline document, no abstract in Crossref/PubMed/OpenAlex; only a garbled acknowledgment fragment ('All experts involved in the development of these guidelines') was recoverable.]

items.ref_text: Shmueli H, Thomas F, Flint N, Setia G, Janjic A, Siegel RJ. Right-sided infective endocarditis 2020: challenges and updates in diagnosis and treatment. J Am Heart Assoc. 2020;9(15):e017293.
items.abstract: Compared with the extensive data on left-sided infective endocarditis (IE), there is much less published information on the features and management of right-sided IE. Right-sided IE accounts for 5% to 10% of all IE cases, and compared with left-sided IE, it is more often associated with intravenous drug use, intracardiac devices, and central venous catheters... we provide an up-to-date overview on the epidemiology, etiology, microbiology, potential locations of infection in the right heart, diagnosis, imaging, common complications, management, and prognosis.
items.note: GATE 2 (full text, OA JAHA review): explicitly states 'despite relatively low in-hospital mortality, right-sided IE is associated with a high risk of recurrence, specifically in IVDU.' Upgraded from PARTIALLY_SUPPORTED after full-text review.

items.ref_text: Leungsuwan K, Vyasabattu M, Arshad H, Abdelfattah A, Heier KR, Arshad S. Prevalence of right-and left-sided endocarditis among intravenous drug use patients at a large academic medical center. Cardiol Res. 2023;14(3):176.
items.abstract: Background: Left-sided infective endocarditis (IE) is increasingly being recognized among intravenous drug use (IVDU) patients... Results: A total of 197 patients were admitted for management of endocarditis. One hundred and fourteen (57.9%) had right-sided endocarditis, 25 (12.7%) had combined left-sided and right-sided endocarditis, and 58 (29.4%) had left-sided endocarditis. Staphylococcus aureus was the most common pathogen. Mortality and inpatient surgical interventions were higher among patients with left-sided endocarditis. Patent foramen ovale (PFO) was the most common shunt found (3.1%)... with PFO being significantly more common among patients with left-sided endocarditis. Conclusion: Right-sided endocarditis continues to be predominant among IVDU patients...
items.note: Ref 7 (Leungsuwan) reports 58/197 (29.4%) left-sided endocarditis -- falls squarely within the claimed 20-35% range.

Upgraded from PARTIALLY_SUPPORTED/NUMBER_CONTRADICTION -- this was a false flag caused by checking only the abstract.

items.abstract: The aim of the study was to compare background characteristics, microbiology and outcome of patients with right-sided and left-sided intravenous drug use (IDU) associated infective endocarditis (IE). A nationwide retrospective study using the Swedish Registry on Infective Endocarditis between 2008 and 2019 was conducted. A total of 586 people with IDU-IE were identified and divided into left-sided (n=204) and right-sided (n=382) IE. The mean age of patients in the left-sided group was 46 years compared to 35 years in the right-sided group, p<0.001. Left-sided IE had a higher proportion of females. Staphylococcus aureus was the causative pathogen in 48% of cases in the left-sided group compared to 88% in the right-sided group. Unadjusted and adjusted long-term survival was better in right-sided IE compared to left-sided IE.
items.note: GATE 2 (full text): EXACT match -- Results state 'the unadjusted survival rates after... 5-year follow-up were... 55%... for left-sided IE' and '84%...' for right-sided. Upgraded from PARTIALLY_SUPPORTED.

items.abstract: The American Heart Association has sponsored both guidelines and scientific statements that address the diagnosis, management, and prevention of infective endocarditis. Management of infective endocarditis in people who inject drugs is complex and requires a unique approach in all aspects of care... The transthoracic modality is the recommended first-line approach but may only be sufficient in patients where the probability of IE is low and/or with clear acoustic windows, especially in patients with right-sided IE. items.abstract: BACKGROUND: Aortic root abscess (ARA) is a severe and life-threatening complication of infective endocarditis (IE)... OBJECTIVES: ...evaluate postoperative outcomes and identify predictors of in-hospital mortality in patients with IE complicated by ARA. RESULTS: Eight observational studies comprising 2266 patients (637 with ARA, 1629 without ARA) were included. ARA was significantly associated with higher in-hospital mortality (15.7% vs. 12.8%; RR 1.65...), late mortality (21.4% vs. 20.2%; RR 1.44...), and IE recurrence (10.6% vs. 4.8%; RR 2.15...). No significant difference in heart failure incidence was found (RR 1.02, 95% CI 0.86-1.21, P=0.82). Renal failure and Staphylococcus aureus infection were independently associated with higher in-hospital mortality.
items.note: Ref 36's abstract confirms higher in-hospital mortality (RR 1.65), late mortality (RR 1.44), and IE recurrence (RR 2.15) with aortic root abscess -- all matching. However, the claim of 'greater rates of multi-organ failure' is not supported; the abstract explicitly states 'No significant difference in heart failure incidence was found' (RR 1.02, p=0.82), which is the closest outcome reported and points the opposite direction from what's claimed.

items.abstract: Infective endocarditis (IE) is a deadly disease, constituting both diagnostic and treatment challenges. A positive outcome requires rapid and accurate diagnosis, and for that, echocardiography unequivocally remains the cornerstone. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) and transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) have complementary roles... The transthoracic modality is the recommended first-line approach but may only be sufficient in patients where the probability of IE is low and/or with clear acoustic windows, especially in patients with right-sided IE. The transesophageal modality is superior to TTE in most aspects and is recommended for all other patients... Cardiac computed tomography (CT) performs better than TEE in the detection of abscess or pseudoaneurysm... [text truncated before specific sensitivity percentages]
items.note: Ref 35's available abstract text confirms TTE is the recommended first-line approach but is truncated before giving specific sensitivity percentages.

items.sentence: By contrast, TEE offers significantly greater sensitivity, approaching 100% for native valves [35].

items.sentence: In fact, guidelines recommend early TEE in any IE case with high risk features or equivocal TTE findings [35].

items.sentence: For example, socioeconomic deprivation has been associated with higher in-hospital mortality and adverse events among patients hospitalized for IE in a recent large national analysis in the United States [38].
items.ref_text: Abdallah N, Ismayl M, Mohamoud A, Samra M, Goldsweig AM. Socioeconomic disparities in In-hospital outcomes and readmission rates among patients hospitalized with infective endocarditis. Heart lung. 2025;74:206-10.
items.abstract: BACKGROUND: Contemporary data elucidates an association between adverse outcomes and low socioeconomic status (SES) in patients with cardiovascular related hospitalizations... OBJECTIVES: To assess the impact of SES on hospitalized IE cases... RESULTS: Among 41,175 IE hospitalizations, 24,581 (59.7%) included patients with low income. Low income was associated with higher odds of in-hospital mortality (aOR 1.21, 95% CI 1.04-1.41, p=0.012), 30-day readmissions..., 90-day readmissions..., sepsis..., MCS use... and lower odds of stroke... compared with high-income.
items.note: Exact match: ref 38 (Abdallah) reports low income associated with higher in-hospital mortality (aOR 1.21) and higher 30-/90-day readmissions and sepsis, in a 41,175-patient US national database analysis.

items.sentence: Without addressing these broader issues, even perfect diagnostic practice may fail to translate into better outcomes for many IVDUs [39].
items.ref_text: Parashar S, Chan K, Milan D, et al. The impact of unstable housing on emergency department use in a cohort of HIV-positive people in a Canadian setting. AIDS Care. 2014;26(1):53-64.
items.abstract: The social-structural challenges experienced by people living with HIV (PHA) have been shown to contribute to increased use of the emergency department (ED). This study identified factors associated with frequent and nonurgent ED use within a cohort of people accessing antiretroviral therapy (ART) in a Canadian setting... Unstable housing was independently associated with ED use (AOR=1.94...), having three or more ED visits... and being triaged as nonurgent... Frequent and nonurgent use of the ED in this setting is associated with conditions requiring interventions at the social-structural level.
items.note: Ref 39 (Parashar) studies unstable housing and ED use in an HIV-positive cohort in Canada -- a different disease population entirely (not IE, not necessarily IVDU-specific). It supports the general 'structural/housing barriers matter' theme by analogy but is topically distant from the citing sentence's specific claim about IVDU-IE diagnostic practice.

items.sentence: Left-sided vegetations have access to the systemic circulation and are far more likely to embolize (to brain, kidneys, spleen, etc.), precipitate heart failure, and cause irreversible valvular destruction [30].
items.ref_text: Moss R, Munt B. Injection drug use and right sided endocarditis. Heart. 2003;89(5):577-81.
items.abstract: [Review/editorial-style article; OpenAlex-derived text is an excerpt of the opening paragraphs, not a structured abstract, truncated with '...'] ...Staphylococcal endocarditis in injection drug users is now the dominant form of the disease in many urban communities... At our institution..., 63% of 116 hospitalisations between 1994 and 2000 for IE were in injection drug users. Right sided endocarditis accounts for 10% of all IE in population based surveys and a higher proportion of IE in injection drug users... Among injection drug users presenting with fever, 13% will have echocardiographic evidence of IE. If injection drug users with bacteraemia... up to 41% will have evidence of IE. [text cuts off before any '14% left-sided' figure]
items.note: Not confirmable from ref 30's truncated available excerpt.
